results.possibleIdentity: Nicholas (Nick) Curtis (born 1948) is an American graphic artist and prolific type designer whose career began in the late 1960s. Initially based in East Dallas, he got his start designing freelance rock posters and concert poster lettering during the Art Nouveau and psychedelic revival era of the late 1960s. Over several decades, his work spanned multiple mediums including print, audiovisual (A/V) production, broadcast television (notably at an NBC affiliate), and early computer graphics. He is best known for founding the independent type foundry 'Nick's Fonts' in 1997, through which he has released over a thousand digital typefaces, many of which are revivals of historical 19th and 20th-century lettering. His career is characterized by a strong interest in the intersection of commercial art and emerging technology, having worked at a computer company and in A/V production, fields closely aligned with the mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Curtis is also a Resident Associate of the Smithsonian Institution and a Registered Researcher with the Library of Congress. In 2014, he donated a significant collection of approximately 300 books on typography and graphic design history to the University of Virginia's Small Special Collections Library.

results.possibleIdentity: Patricia "Pat" Sloane (1934–2001) was an American painter, filmmaker, author, and educator active from the late 1950s through the 1990s. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, she was deeply involved in the New York art scene both as a practitioner and a critic. As a painter, she was associated with the 10th Street Galleries (specifically the Camino Gallery) and the New York School, later moving toward Abstract Expressionist and geometric styles. Her experimental film work includes the 1970 16mm film "A Knee Ad," which she described as a non-narrative cinematic statement on motion. Sloane was also a significant figure in the study of color theory, receiving a Guggenheim Fellowship in 1974 for her research. Her association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the broader art-and-technology movement is evidenced by her critical writing on emerging media; for example, she wrote "Video Revolution" for Art and Artists in 1972, discussing the work of Nam June Paik. She held a PhD from New York University and was a long-time professor of fine arts at New York City Technical College (CUNY).

results.possibleIdentity: David Curtis is a British filmmaker, curator, and historian of experimental cinema who was a central figure in the London avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. In 1967, he became the film programmer at the Drury Lane Arts Lab, a pioneering alternative arts center. Following its closure, he was a co-founder and programmer for the New Arts Lab (also known as the Institute for Research in Art and Technology or IRAT) from 1969 to 1971. IRAT was conceptually aligned with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, focusing on the integration of art, computers, video, and cybernetic theatre. Curtis played a key role in the formation of the London Film-Makers' Co-op and authored 'Experimental Cinema: A Fifty Year History' (1971), one of the first major surveys of the field. He later served as the Senior Film Officer at the Arts Council of Great Britain from 1977 to 2000 and founded the British Artists’ Film & Video Study Collection at Central Saint Martins. His work as a 'general enabler' and curator has documented the genealogy of art-and-technology labs that emerged during that era.

results.possibleIdentity: Richard Lowenberg (born 1946 in Haifa, Israel) is an American-based artist, designer, and eco-cultural activist whose work has focused on the intersection of art, science, technology, and ecology since the late 1960s. He studied architecture and design at the Pratt Institute in Brooklyn from 1964 to 1968. During this formative period in New York, he was deeply involved in the experimental art scene and served as a student assistant to Robert Rauschenberg during the landmark '9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering' (1966), a seminal event organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1971, he was a founding member of the influential media art space The Kitchen in New York and initiated the first electronic media arts program at Pratt Institute. Lowenberg's career is marked by pioneering bio-sensing and interspecies communication projects, such as his Bio-Arts Lab (established in 1973) and his collaborative video performance 'The Secret Life of Plants' (1976), which utilized plant bio-signals to generate music and visuals. Throughout the 1970s and 1980s, he conducted extensive collaborations with NASA, including 'Gravitational-Field-Day' (1979), which involved dancers in astronaut training simulations. His practice continues to evolve through initiatives like the 1st-Mile Institute and the SARC (Scientists/Artists Research Collaborations), emphasizing the 'ecology of the information environment.'

results.possibleIdentity: Walter Wright is an interdisciplinary artist, video pioneer, and software engineer who has been active since the late 1960s. He is primarily known for his early experiments in computer animation, video synthesis, and electronic music. Wright began creating electronic images and sound at the University of Waterloo in 1966. In the early 1970s, he worked as a video animator for Computer Image Corporation (CIC) in New York City, where he utilized the Scanimate analog computer system to create video graphics. During this period, he was a key collaborator with experimental artist Ed Emshwiller on the pioneering video works 'Thermogenesis' (1971) and 'Scapemates' (1972), the latter of which was the first computer-generated video nominated for an Emmy Award. Wright was closely associated with the Experimental Television Center (ETC), where he served as the second artist-in-residence (following Nam June Paik) from 1973 to 1976. In this role, he toured galleries, schools, and media centers with the Paik/Abe Video Synthesizer, demonstrating real-time video performance. He also served as an associate director at The Kitchen in New York, where he organized weekly 'Open Screenings' for video artists. His work was heavily inspired by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) collective and the technological developments at Bell Laboratories. In his later career, Wright co-founded the 119 Gallery (originally 911 Gallery) and developed various visual instruments and software, such as the Video Shredder and the AVSynth.

results.possibleIdentity: Ken Marsh (born 1940) is an artist, author, and video pioneer who was active in the New York art and technology scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. Initially working with light shows, he became a central figure in the early video art movement. In 1970, he co-founded the People's Video Theater (PVT) in New York City with Elliot Glass, focusing on community video, man-on-the-street interviews, and 'video mediation' to resolve social conflicts. He was a contributor to the influential video journal 'Radical Software' and later established Woodstock Community Video (WCV) in 1973. Marsh is also recognized for his technical and artistic expertise, having authored the seminal handbook 'Independent Video: A Complete Guide to the Physics, Operation, and Application of the New Television for the Student, the Artist and for Community TV' in 1974. He has been associated with the circles of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and other pioneers of electronic and participatory art, where his work emphasized the use of technology as a tool for social change and communication. In addition to his video work, he has been described as a painter and graphic designer.

results.possibleIdentity: Paul Green (born Paul Eliot Green, 1894–1981) was a Pulitzer Prize-winning playwright and activist who was active through the late 1960s and 1970s. He is credited as a pioneer of 'symphonic outdoor drama,' a form of theater that integrated technology, including sophisticated lighting and sound effects, into an organic artistic whole. His work, such as 'The Lost Colony' (performed annually since 1937) and 'The House of Connelly,' is often cited in the context of early experiments in theater technology and spectacle. His belief in 'every man as an artist' and his focus on large-scale collaborations between different disciplines align with the ethos of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was active as a professor of Dramatic Art at the University of North Carolina and a major figure in the North Carolina art scene during the specified period.

Paul Green was a visual artist active in the Los Angeles area during the early 1970s. His work was featured in the 1971 survey exhibition 'Color' in Palo Alto, which showcased selected work from 19 Los Angeles-based artists working in media such as ceramics, fiber, metal, wood, and glass. He was grouped with prominent California ceramic and craft-based artists like Beatrice Wood, John Mason, and Laura Andreson. Given his presence in the Los Angeles art scene at a time when Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) had an active branch there, he is a likely candidate for the artist associated with the organization.

Paul Green (often Paul E. Green Jr., 1927–2012) was a scientist and engineer who worked for IBM and Bell Laboratories and was directly associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He participated in E.A.T.'s 'Utopia Q&A' project in 1971, a pioneering international telecommunications project that connected participants in different cities via telex stations. He was an expert in optical networking and fiber optics, representing the 'engineer/scientist' collaborator crucial to E.A.T.'s mission of bridging the gap between art and technology.

results.possibleIdentity: Joan Webster Price (born 1931, Camden, New Jersey) is an American abstract artist, sculptor, and educator known for her work in geometric abstraction and her pioneering integration of technology and art. She is a Professor Emerita at the City College of New York (CCNY), where she taught in the Art Department for many years. Her artistic career is marked by a diverse range of media, including printmaking, drawing, and large-scale sculpture. Price is particularly recognized for her collaborations with Herbert Price in creating 'solar sculptures'—stainless-steel structures that utilize solar panels or hot water systems to harness and objectify solar energy, reflecting the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement to bridge the gap between artists and engineers. She has been an active member of the American Abstract Artists (AAA) collective, contributing to several of their landmark print portfolios such as the 50th and 60th Anniversary collections. Her works, including pieces like 'Tower Fantasy' (1987) and 'To Mars and Beyond' (1997), are held in the permanent collections of prestigious institutions such as the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York, the Smithsonian American Art Museum, and the Phillips Collection.

results.possibleIdentity: John Nagle is a physicist and researcher who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and later at Carnegie Mellon University. In historical accounts of the MIT Center for Theoretical Physics, Nagle is described as having "a little bit of the poet and artist" in him. He is noted for using early computer technology to create graphic "embellishments" and visualizations of complex scientific concepts, such as nuclear structure and particle physics branches. This work, which utilized computer-aided design to illustrate scientific theory, aligns with the objectives of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which fostered collaborations between artists and scientists during that era.

John Nagle (also referred to as John J. Nagle) is a computer scientist and inventor who has been active in the fields of computer graphics and networking since the late 1970s. He is most famous for developing 'Nagle's algorithm' for TCP/IP networks in 1984. However, his work has often crossed into artistic and creative domains, specifically through computer animation and simulation. He is the creator of 'Animats' and produced technical demos like 'Spiral Stair Fall' (2001), which explored ragdoll physics and procedural animation. His background as an engineer who utilizes technology for visual expression makes him a potential match for the technical collaborators associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

John Hailes Flood 'Gaffer' Nagle (1913–2009) was a prominent Australian judge and patron of the arts who was active throughout the 1960s and 1970s. While his primary profession was in law—serving as a justice of the Supreme Court of New South Wales from 1960 to 1983—he was deeply embedded in the art world as a trustee and chairman of the Art Gallery of New South Wales (1977–1980). He was a close friend of the famous Australian painter Russell Drysdale and published a biography of the artist in 1979. His role as a high-level administrator and patron during the 1970s involved facilitating the acquisition and promotion of contemporary art, which may have brought him into contact with international organizations like E.A.T.
